---
title: "Go deeper"
subtitle: "More to try – if you finish early, or want to push further. All self-paced, all on your own work."
---

These extend the day's ideas without adding to the main flow. Each is a technique you can run in five minutes on something real. They all do the same thing the day does – keep your judgement in charge – just from different angles.

Everything here is drawn from the free companion book, [*Conversation, Not Delegation*](https://michael-borck.github.io/conversation-not-delegation/).

## Push back: VET your AI

The **trust tool** tells you *when* to keep a human in the loop. **VET** tells you *how* – three questions to run on any AI output that matters. The most dangerous output isn't the obvious hallucination; it's the one that sounds completely right.

> **Verify, Explain, Test.** If you can't verify it, explain it, and test it, you're not done yet.

| AI says… | **V**erify | **E**xplain | **T**est |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Studies show…" | Find the actual studies | Can I explain the mechanism? | What about a different context? |
| "The optimal figure is X" | Where's that number from? | Why X, specifically? | What happens if I change a variable? |
| "This method is 95% effective" | Source? | What does 95% mean here? | Under what conditions? |

- **Verify – can I find this independently?** Check sources, cross-reference claims, look up citations. AI fabricates references routinely because generating a plausible citation is the same process as generating any text. A beautifully formatted reference that doesn't exist is the single most common failure.
- **Explain – can I say this in my own words?** If you can't rephrase it without leaning on the AI's exact wording, you're holding words, not understanding. Rewrite it; teach it to a colleague. Where you struggle to rephrase is where your gap is.
- **Test – does this hold up under scrutiny?** Play devil's advocate, check the edge cases, ask "what if?" Change a variable. Fragile answers break; robust ones flex.

Use VET hardest when the output contains claims you'll rely on, a decision hangs on it, or it'll be shared with people who assume you checked. A casual brainstorm gets a light pass; a client deliverable gets the full treatment. [Full chapter →](https://michael-borck.github.io/conversation-not-delegation/chapters/vet-your-ai.html)

## Better conversations

The shape of the conversation changes the output. A single prompt gets you an answer; a conversation gets you understanding.

### Devil's advocate
Make the AI argue *against* your idea before you commit. It surfaces the weakest assumptions – the ones you'd rather find now than after you've decided.
> *"Argue against this plan as a sceptical, experienced critic. Find its three weakest assumptions, and for each, one question that would need a good answer and one thing that would have to be true for it to survive."*
**Teaches:** the edge isn't in the idea you like; it's in surviving the challenge you didn't want to hear. *(A ready-made version is in the [RTCF toolkit](tools/rtcf-toolkit.html) → Templates.)*

### Boardroom perspectives
Turn one decision into a panel. Ask the AI to take several distinct expert perspectives – strategist, finance, operator, ethicist – and give each a recommendation.
> *"Act as a small advisory panel: a strategist, a finance lead, and an operator. Analyse this decision from each perspective. End each with a single recommendation."*
**Teaches:** you decide which voice to weight – that weighting is your judgement.

### The two-chat workflow
Separate thinking from building across **two sessions**.
- **Session 1 – Thinking Chat:** explore, challenge assumptions, follow tangents, clarify what your *actual* question is. Messy by design; disposable.
- **In between – the magic:** read what emerged, decide what matters, discard what doesn't, write a focused brief. You don't copy-paste; you *curate*. **That curation is where your judgement lives.**
- **Session 2 – Build Chat:** produce the output from the brief. Better input, better output.

Neither session has the full picture. Only you hold both – that's the real meaning of "human in the loop." *Teaching example: Chat 1 – brainstorm what an assessment should test and where students stumble; Chat 2 – draft the task sheet from your one-paragraph brief.*

### The handover prompt
Long chats drift and lose the thread. Before you close one, ask the AI to package the context so you can pick it up clean.
> *"Write a handover prompt that captures everything important from this conversation – context, decisions made, where we left off – so I can paste it into a new chat and continue."*
Save it, start fresh, paste it in. **You keep the thinking; you lose the noise.** Great for multi-session work (a grant draft, a policy, a unit redesign).

### The conversation loop
The shape underneath all of the above – four stages, one rule: **you stay in the conversation.**

1. **Brainstorm** – define your real question, not your attempted solution.
2. **Ideate** – go wide; explore angles you hadn't considered.
3. **Iterate** – push back, refine, redirect. *"The tone's wrong." / "You're solving the wrong problem – the constraint is budget, not timeline."* This is where the output becomes yours.
4. **Amplify** – edit, restructure, cut, bring it into your voice. Put your name on it.
5. **Repeat** – loop again until your thinking has landed.

The loop works because **your expertise + AI's breadth = amplified thinking**, and the bottleneck is your thinking, not the model. A better model won't fix a lazy prompt; skipping iteration won't be saved by speed. [Full chapter →](https://michael-borck.github.io/conversation-not-delegation/chapters/the-conversation-loop.html)

:::: {.callout-note}
## One thread through all of these
Every technique here keeps you from the easiest mistake: accepting fluent, generic output. Verify it, split thinking from building, argue against it, iterate. Each is a different way of doing the same thing – **adding your judgement instead of just adding context.**
::::
